text: Dr. Eric Smith utilizes the Exide DaVinci Surgical System for a robotic sleeve gastrectomy. The camera is positioned to the left of the umbilicus, with four trocars placed above it. The surgeon begins dissection at the mid portion of the stomach, moving towards the upper fundus. The fourth robotic arm pulls up while the first arm pulls down and laterally, exposing the left crus and angle of His. The surgeon then switches arm positions to mobilize the fat pad off the angle of His, allowing the fundus to drop down.

The surgeon uses robotic arms to manipulate the stomach, focusing on the posterior aspect of the fundus. The first arm is positioned lower to navigate through filmy adhesions near the pancreas, revealing a dimple in the fat that guides the dissection into an avascular plane. The surgeon lifts and laterally spreads the stomach to separate fatty tissue from the posterior fundus, progressing towards the left crus. The fourth arm is used to grasp and mobilize the fat pad, ensuring the staple line remains tension-free and does not tuck under the left crus. The surgeon checks for any remaining tissue near the gastroesophageal junction and the base of the left crus.

The surgeon mobilizes the greater curvature of the stomach, sealing vessels using the E-one-hundred generator, and progresses towards the pylorus. The camera is repositioned to the right trocar, and a forty French bougie is inserted. The SureForm stapler is used with a green load to ensure a tight and hemostatic staple line.

The surgeon slides the stapler behind the specimen side of the stomach, lifting the tissue up and laterally to fire staples towards the angle of His, staying lateral to avoid the esophagus. The specimen is placed aside, and the staple line is sutured using a 2-0 absorbable V-lock suture. The omentum is sewn back to the staple line to promote healing and maintain the sleeve's straightness, with the final throw locked for additional hemostasis.
